Consciousness Science Machine Minds Moral Status
Glossary About
Medieval Latin Scholasticism

William of Ockham

c. 1287 – 1347

Nominalism applied to mind: intuitive cognition, concepts, and the razor.

William of Ockham does not offer a single modern treatise “on the mind.” What he leaves is something at once more scattered and more systematic: a set of positions on anima, intellectus, voluntas, notitia, conceptus, signum, suppositio, divine cognition, angelic cognition, and the epistemic status of human psychology. Reconstructed carefully, these positions yield a distinctive philosophy of mind: ontologically austere, act-centered, semantically sophisticated, directly oriented toward singulars, skeptical of unnecessary psychological machinery, strongly voluntarist in moral psychology, and explicitly theological in its modal architecture.

Ockham’s philosophy of mind is not reducible to “nominalism.” He is a fourteenth-century Franciscan theologian and philosopher, usually dated c. 1287–1347, whose work ranges over logic, metaphysics, natural philosophy, theology, political theory, and epistemology. The Stanford Encyclopedia’s updated account rightly treats him as a major late-medieval figure whose importance extends well beyond the nominalist label.

The central thesis of this report is this: for Ockham, minds are not inner theaters populated by representational intermediaries such as species, common natures, or abstract objects. A mind is a subject of acts, habits, cognitions, judgments, volitions, passions, and natural signs. In human beings, this subject is complicated by the plurality of bodily, sensitive, and intellective forms; in angels and separated souls, it is clarified by the absence of sensation; in God, it reaches a limiting case in which “intellect,” “will,” “knowledge,” and “volition” name one simple divine reality under different concepts. Theology is therefore not an external appendix to Ockham’s philosophy of mind. It supplies some of its most important boundary cases.

1. The basic ontological frame: substances, qualities, signs, and acts

Ockham’s psychology belongs to a broader metaphysical program. He is famous for reducing ontological commitments: where possible, one should avoid multiplying entities beyond what explanatory need requires. This does not mean that he denies mental reality. On the contrary, cognitions, volitions, habits, and passions are real items in the soul. But they are not to be explained by postulating additional intermediaries unless such intermediaries are necessary.

A Spanish-language philosophical encyclopedia summarizes the metaphysical background well: Ockham admits two broad classes of absolute realities, substances and qualities; beyond these, he is reluctant to posit further entities as really distinct constituents of the world. In psychology, this principle has immediate consequences. Mental acts can be real qualities of the soul, but universal natures, intelligible species, and internal quasi-objects are suspect unless they do indispensable work.

This is why Ockham’s philosophy of mind is not materialist, but it is also not Platonist or representationalist in the strong scholastic sense. The mind is real; its acts are real; but the contents of thought do not require a realm of universal entities, nor do acts of cognition require species as mediating likenesses. In a primary text from the Summa logicae, Ockham explicitly discusses whether the “intention,” “concept,” or “passion of the soul” is a fictive object, a quality in the soul, or the very act of understanding. His preferred mature tendency is toward the act-theory: if an act of understanding can perform the semantic work, there is no need to posit another entity in addition to it.

That point is foundational. A modern reconstruction of Ockham’s philosophy of mind should begin from acts, not from inner objects.

2. The human being: not Cartesian dualism, but plural hylomorphism

Ockham is not a Cartesian dualist. He does not begin with an immaterial thinking substance over against an extended body. His anthropology remains scholastic and hylomorphic: a human being is a composite involving matter and form. Yet he is not a simple Aristotelian-Thomist unitarist about the human substantial form. He tends toward a plurality of forms in the human being.

A major Spanish study of Ockham’s philosophical anthropology emphasizes that, for Ockham, the definition of the human being cannot be exhausted by simply saying “body plus intellective soul.” Ockham recognizes at least a form of corporeity, a sensitive soul, and an intellective soul. This plurality matters for philosophy of mind because it lets Ockham distinguish the subject of sensory acts from the subject of intellectual acts. Sense perception, imagination, and sensitive appetite belong to the sensitive soul; understanding and rational willing belong to the intellective soul.

One of Ockham’s arguments for distinguishing sensitive and intellective soul is psychological conflict. A human being can desire something at the level of sensitive appetite while rejecting it at the level of rational will. Such conflict is easier to explain if sensitive and intellective powers are not merely nominal differences within a single undifferentiated form. A Spanish article by Gamboa similarly stresses that, for Ockham, perception, imagination, and understanding are assigned to different subjects: the sensitive soul enables perception and imagination, while the intellective soul enables acts of understanding. If they were the same in the relevant respect, the difference between perceiving and understanding would be harder to preserve.

This makes Ockham’s philosophy of mind structurally layered. There is not just “mind” and “body.” There is corporeal organization, sensitive life, and intellective life. The human person is a unity, but not a metaphysically simple unity.

At the same time, Ockham is strikingly cautious about what can be demonstrated philosophically. He does not deny the immortality of the soul as a matter of Christian doctrine. Rather, he denies that natural reason can demonstrate that the intellective soul is an immaterial, incorruptible form of the body. The Spanish study just cited quotes Ockham’s position sharply: doctrines of this kind are believed, not demonstratively known by unaided reason. This is one of the places where Ockham diverges most sharply from Aquinas. Aquinas thinks one can give philosophical arguments for the immateriality and incorruptibility of the human intellective soul; Ockham is far less confident.

The result is not a reduction of the soul to the body. It is epistemic discipline. Ockham distinguishes what Christian theology affirms from what natural philosophy can prove. In reconstructing his philosophy of mind, one should therefore say: Ockham accepts the intellective soul as immaterial and immortal by faith, but he refuses to treat that status as philosophically demonstrable in the way Aquinas does.

3. Intellect, will, and soul: no real distinction of faculties

One of Ockham’s most important and easily misunderstood positions is his denial of a real distinction between the intellective soul and its faculties. For many scholastics, intellect and will are really distinct powers of the rational soul. Ockham is more austere. He treats “intellect” and “will” as connotative or denominative terms that signify the same intellective soul while connoting different acts.

A German-language study gives the point with useful precision: Ockham rejects a real distinction between the intellective soul and its faculties. The intellective soul is called “intellect” insofar as it can think, and “will” insofar as it can will. The terms signify the same undivided intellective soul but connote distinct acts. They are coextensive without being synonymous. The Summa logicae makes the same semantic point with examples such as “intellect,” “will,” and “soul”: these terms can be verified of the same thing while differing in what they connote.

This gives us an Ockhamist theory of faculties: faculties are not additional inner objects or sub-substances. They are the soul considered under act-related descriptions. The same soul is the intellect insofar as it understands; the same soul is the will insofar as it wills.

This has three systematic consequences.

First, Ockham’s philosophy of mind is not modular in the sense of positing multiple internal substances or quasi-substances. The rational soul is the subject of intellectual and volitional acts.

Second, his semantics explains his metaphysics. “Intellect” and “will” differ not because there are two inner beings, but because the terms have different connotative meanings.

Third, this position makes his voluntarism subtler. He is not saying that the will is an ontologically separate faculty that wars against the intellect. He is saying that the same rational soul can perform acts of understanding and acts of willing, and that acts of willing are not determined by acts of understanding.

4. Ockham’s rejection of species: cognition without representational intermediaries

Ockham’s psychology is deeply anti-mediational. He rejects the need for sensible and intelligible species, at least as real psychological intermediaries required to explain cognition. He remains broadly Aristotelian in vocabulary, but he strips out much of the representational apparatus that earlier scholastics thought necessary. The Stanford Encyclopedia summarizes the point: Ockham rejects sensory and intellectual species as unnecessary, and he appeals both to parsimony and to the lack of experiential support for them.

This rejection is crucial. For Aquinas, cognition of external objects typically involves species: forms received without matter in the senses and then intelligible species in the intellect. For Ockham, this machinery is not needed. The object itself, the cognitive power, and the resulting act suffice. Cognition is not explained by inserting a likeness between mind and world.

The resulting position is often called direct realism, though the label is modern and should be used cautiously. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy describes Ockham’s view as a form of direct realist empiricism: intuitive cognition involves a causal link between world and mind, without the mediation of species, while still allowing room for error and for skeptical scenarios. The label “direct realism” is helpful as long as one remembers that Ockham’s theory is not a modern naïve realism. He allows for divine causation of cognition without ordinary objects, and he distinguishes the act of cognition from the judgment formed on its basis.

The deepest reason for rejecting species is not merely “simplicity” in the superficial sense. It is that species threaten to obscure the explanatory role of the object itself. For Ockham, when a singular thing is naturally known intuitively, the singular thing is the relevant cause and terminus of cognition. The mind does not first know a species and then infer an object. It knows the object through an act caused, in the natural order, by that object.

5. Intuitive and abstractive cognition

The distinction between intuitive and abstractive cognition is the heart of Ockham’s epistemology and one of the most important pieces of his philosophy of mind.

Intuitive cognition is cognition by which the intellect can know whether a thing exists or does not exist, and by which it can ground evident contingent judgments. Abstractive cognition is cognition that abstracts from the existence or non-existence of the object; it allows thinking, remembering, generalizing, and conceptualizing without presenting the object as existent here and now. The Stanford Encyclopedia gives the rough modern analogy: intuitive cognition is like perception, while abstractive cognition is like imagination, memory, or generalizing; but the analogy must be qualified because Ockham allows intellectual intuitive cognition of individuals.

A Spanish philosophical account states the distinction in Ockham’s own framework. Both intuitive and abstractive incomplex cognitions can have the same singular object. The difference is not that intuition is of particulars while abstraction is of universals. Rather, intuitive cognition is naturally suited to evident judgments about the object’s existence or non-existence, whereas abstractive cognition abstracts from such existential presence.

This point is often missed. Ockham is not saying: sensation gives particulars and intellect gives universals. He is saying that the intellect itself can have intuitive cognition of singulars. This is one of his decisive breaks with more traditional Aristotelian epistemology. The intellect can know the singular as singular.

The natural cognitive sequence, in the case of ordinary human cognition of external sensible things, is roughly this: a singular sensible thing causes a sensory act; the sensory act is associated with an intellectual intuitive cognition of the same singular; the intellect can then form a contingent judgment, such as “this thing exists” or “this thing is white”; from such acts, abstractive cognitions and habits can arise. The Spanish account of Ockham’s theory describes just such a process: sensible thing, sensation, singular intellectual intuition, judgment, abstractive act, habit or memory.

This theory has large implications. Ockham’s mind is not primarily a universalizing machine. Its first epistemic contact with the world is singular, direct, and act-based. Universality enters at the level of signs, concepts, and supposition, not because the world contains common natures over and above individual things.

6. Intellectual intuition of one’s own mental acts

Ockham extends intuitive cognition beyond external objects. The intellect can intuitively know its own acts and certain acts of the will. This is central for self-knowledge.

Susan Brower-Toland’s work on Ockham’s intellectual intuition emphasizes that Ockham argues from self-knowledge: we know not only sensible things but also our own thoughts, volitions, pleasures, sadnesses, and similar mental states. Such knowledge is not sensory; nor is it merely inferential; nor is it abstractive if it gives us evident knowledge of present acts. Therefore, there must be intuitive cognition at the level of the intellect.

This gives Ockham a medieval theory of introspection. The intellect has a reflexive intuitive cognition of its own first-order acts. I know that I am thinking, willing, rejoicing, or grieving by an intellectual act directed toward my own mental act. The object of this higher-order act is not an external apple, stone, or color, but the first-order act itself.

The Spanish Philosophica entry makes a similar point: the intellect can intuit intelligible acts such as acts of understanding, willing, pleasure, and sadness; this supports a doctrine of self-knowledge through intuitive cognition of subjective states.

This should not be inflated into a Cartesian doctrine of transparent self-consciousness. Ockham does not think that all aspects of the self are known with certainty. Nor does he think that the soul’s essence is directly known in all its metaphysical status. What is directly and intuitively known are acts: this act of understanding, this act of willing, this delight, this sadness. Self-knowledge begins from mental events, not from an immediate grasp of the soul’s metaphysical nature.

In contemporary terms, Ockham is much stronger on state-consciousness than on substance-consciousness. We have privileged access to some of our acts; we do not thereby have demonstrative metaphysical knowledge of the soul’s essence, immateriality, or immortality.

7. Intuition, existence, non-existence, and divine omnipotence

Ockham’s theory of intuitive cognition becomes especially subtle when he asks whether there can be intuitive cognition of a non-existent thing. In the natural order, intuitive cognition is caused by the object and is tied to the object’s presence or existence. But Ockham also allows that God, by omnipotence, could cause in a mind an intuitive cognition of an object that does not exist.

This is not a casual skeptical trick. It is a theological modal claim. If cognition is an absolute quality of the mind, and if God can produce any absolute quality that does not involve contradiction, then God can produce a cognitive act without the ordinary created cause. A French discussion of Ockham’s intuition and abstraction captures the issue well: naturally, cognition is caused by the real singular object, but divine power can produce a cognition without the object existing. The Spanish Philosophica entry also notes that Ockham admits, against some expectations, that divine omnipotence could cause intuitive cognition of a non-existing thing.

This has generated a major interpretive dispute. Does Ockham’s allowance of intuitive cognition of non-existents undermine the evidential force of intuition? Does it introduce skepticism? Or is it merely a supernatural exception that leaves the natural epistemic order intact? The Cambridge Companion material notes a debate between what may be called the standard reading and alternative interpretations, including Karger’s challenge to the claim that intuitive cognition has an intrinsic privileged connection with evident judgment.

A careful reconstruction should say this. In the natural order, intuitive cognition is the mind’s direct existentially relevant cognition of a singular. In the absolute modal order opened by divine omnipotence, God can produce a similar cognitive act without the object’s ordinary presence. This does not make Ockham a simple skeptic; but it does mean that epistemology is subordinated to a broader theology of divine power. The created mind’s natural reliability is real, but it is not metaphysically ultimate.

8. Mental language: thought as natural signification

Ockham’s philosophy of mind cannot be separated from his theory of mental language. He distinguishes written, spoken, and conceived language. Written and spoken terms are conventional signs; mental terms are concepts or intentions of the soul that naturally signify. In the Summa logicae, he says that conceived terms are “passions of the soul” or mental words that cannot be uttered outwardly, and that spoken words are subordinated to concepts.

The central point is natural signification. Spoken and written words signify by convention. Mental terms signify naturally. Their signification is not changed at will in the way the meaning of a spoken word can be changed by linguistic convention.

This is why Ockham’s “concepts” should not be understood as private images, Platonic forms, or inert inner objects. In his mature act-theory, a concept is an act of understanding functioning as a natural sign. That act can enter into mental propositions. Mental propositions are the immediate bearers of judgment, inference, and science.

The Stanford Encyclopedia emphasizes that Ockham was among the first medieval philosophers to develop a detailed theory of mental language. Written and spoken language are subordinated to concepts, and mental terms naturally signify rather than signifying by convention. A French study by Alain de Libera/Claude Panaccio/Joël Biard’s interpretive milieu, represented in the Biard source, also stresses that Ockham’s more rigorous presentation grounds the theory in the concept as an act of intellection, while earlier “fictum” language is corrected or abandoned.

The mental-language theory lets Ockham explain universality without universal things. A concept is universal not because it is a universal object, but because it is a sign naturally apt to stand for many singulars. “Human being” as a mental term does not signify a common human nature existing outside the mind. It signifies individual humans in a general way.

This gives Ockham a powerful semantic philosophy of mind. Thinking is not merely having images. Thinking is structured signification. The mind composes and divides mental signs in propositions; judgments are acts involving such propositions; reasoning is structured by the logical relations among them.

There are, however, unresolved interpretive problems. Is Ockham’s mental language a canonical language without synonymy and equivocation? Or can there be mental synonymy? The Stanford Encyclopedia notes the debate, and the Cambridge Companion material reports that scholars such as Panaccio have challenged the older view that Ockham straightforwardly excludes all mental synonymy. The safest formulation is that Ockham wants mental language to be more naturally structured and less conventional than spoken language, but the exact extent to which it eliminates synonymy remains debated.

9. Supposition, connotation, and the semantics of mental life

Ockham’s philosophy of mind is inseparable from his logical semantics. Terms signify; in propositions they supposit, or stand for things. Some terms signify absolutely; others connote something. The distinction is indispensable for psychology.

The terms “soul,” “intellect,” and “will” are especially revealing. They can stand for the same thing—the intellective soul—while connoting different acts or capacities. “Intellect” connotes acts of understanding; “will” connotes acts of willing. This lets Ockham avoid positing really distinct faculties while preserving real differences among act-types.

The same semantic strategy becomes decisive in theology. Terms such as “divine intellect,” “divine will,” “divine knowledge,” and “divine volition” do not name really distinct parts or faculties in God. They stand for the same simple divine reality while differing in connotation and in their creaturely analogues. Ockham’s philosophy of mind thus requires a semantic theory robust enough to handle both human psychology and divine simplicity.

This is one reason why “philosophy of mind” in Ockham cannot be isolated from logic. His solution to psychological and theological puzzles often takes the form: do not multiply things; distinguish instead the ways terms signify and supposit.

10. Judgment, evidence, and scientific knowledge

Ockham distinguishes simple apprehension from judgment. Intuitive and abstractive cognitions are incomplex acts; propositions are complex; assent and dissent occur at the level of judgment. An intuitive cognition of a white thing can ground the evident judgment “this thing is white,” but the cognition and the judgment are not identical.

Science, for Ockham, is not a relation to an abstract object called “science.” It is a habit or quality in the soul. The Spanish Philosophica account notes that science is treated as a quality or habit of the soul. This is consistent with his broader ontology: stable intellectual dispositions are real qualities, but they do not require positing abstract entities beyond acts and habits.

The object of scientific knowledge is often analyzed propositionally. The French Biard source notes Ockham’s claim in the Expositio in libros Physicorum that the object of science is the proposition, a thesis that triggered medieval debates. This is important for philosophy of mind because it means that intellectual achievement is not simply acquaintance with things. It involves propositional structure, assent, evidence, and habits of demonstration.

Ockham’s account of mind therefore has at least three levels: simple acts of cognition, propositional acts or complexes, and habitual dispositions. A mind is not merely a stream of sensations; it is a structured signifying and judging subject.

11. The will: freedom, indifference, and non-determination by intellect

Ockham’s moral psychology is among the most radical parts of his philosophy of mind. He denies that the will is determined by the intellect’s judgment of the good. The will is informed by intellect, but it is not necessitated by it. The Stanford Encyclopedia contrasts Ockham with Aquinas here: for Ockham, the will can choose whether to will the ultimate good and can even choose against it; choices are informed by intellect but not determined by intellect.

This is not an ontological dualism of two separate faculties, because, as noted above, Ockham denies a real distinction between intellect and will as faculties. The same rational soul understands and wills. But willing has its own irreducible mode of freedom. The intellect presents, judges, or apprehends; the will can accept, reject, suspend, or choose otherwise.

This gives Ockham a strong form of libertarian freedom. Human beings are morally responsible because their volitions are not necessitated by cognition, passions, habits, or external circumstances. He is not saying that choices occur in an informational vacuum. The will acts in relation to what is understood. But intellectual presentation does not determine volitional outcome.

This view has profound implications for a philosophy of mind. It rejects intellectualism: the mind is not governed simply by cognition of the good. It also rejects reduction of volition to appetite or affect. The will is a distinctive mode of rational agency.

12. Passions and emotions: sensitive and volitional affect

Ockham’s treatment of passions further enriches his philosophy of mind. Medieval theories of emotion often distinguish sensory passions from rational or volitional acts. Ockham develops this distinction in a way that reflects his plural psychology.

Vesa Hirvonen’s work on Ockham’s passions, and Dominik Perler’s studies of medieval emotions, emphasize that Ockham treats emotions as intentional and cognitively structured rather than as raw bodily feelings. Perler’s account of medieval emotion theory notes that Ockham and Adam Wodeham analyze sensory and volitional passions in detail: sensory passions have cognitive components and intentional objects, while volitional passions depend on conceptualization and evaluation.

For Ockham, a sensory passion is not merely a physiological disturbance. It involves the sensitive soul’s orientation toward an object under some aspect. Pain, for example, can be considered not only as a raw feeling but as a passion with intentional structure. Volitional passions, by contrast, belong to the rational soul’s willing life. Enjoyment, love, and related affective states can be rooted in acts of will and in conceptual appraisal.

This helps explain psychological conflict. A person can be drawn by sensory appetite and yet rationally reject the object; or a person can rationally will something contrary to sensory inclination. The divided structure of the human soul is reflected in the divided structure of affect.

A comprehensive Ockhamist philosophy of mind must therefore include emotions. They are not incidental. They show that mind is not merely cognition plus choice; it is also affective intentionality, distributed across sensitive and intellective levels.

13. The mind of God: divine simplicity, divine ideas, and theological semantics

The mind of God is not a theological distraction from Ockham’s philosophy of mind. It is one of the system’s limiting cases. Ockham’s treatment of divine cognition clarifies what he thinks cognition can be when freed from creaturely composition, faculties, species, and discursive limitation.

Ockham is committed to divine simplicity. Terms such as “God’s intellect,” “God’s will,” “God’s cognitions,” and “God’s volitions” are many, but they do not name many really distinct divine parts or faculties. They stand for one simple divine being. They are not synonyms, however, because when applied to creatures they correspond to distinct realities or act-types. Kilcullen’s account of Ockham’s philosophical theology states this clearly: many terms referring to God’s will, volitions, intellect, and cognitions stand for one simple being, though they are not synonymous because of their creaturely uses.

This is the theological counterpart of Ockham’s human faculty theory. In human beings, “intellect” and “will” name the same rational soul under different connotations. In God, they name the same absolutely simple divine reality. But in God the identity is stronger: there are not even really distinct acts inhering in a subject as created qualities.

Portuguese scholarship on Ockham’s divine ideas is especially helpful here. Carolina Fernández notes that Ockham reserves the term “Idea” for the psychology of divinity and does not use it as his standard term for human knowledge. She also emphasizes that, for Ockham, “Idea” does not signify a distinct thing; it is a connotative term. This is a crucial anti-Platonic move. Divine ideas are not independent exemplars, not common natures, and not entities mediating between God and creatures.

Ockham rejects the view that God creates by consulting universal natures or Platonic forms. Given his nominalism, there are no real common natures for divine ideas to be. God knows individuals. A screenshoted section of Kilcullen’s discussion of divine ideas presents the Ockhamist point: divine ideas are not distinct natures or forms; God creates individual things according to his knowledge of individuals, including individuals that do not yet exist.

This yields a profound theological philosophy of mind. God’s “mind” is not an infinite storehouse of universal representations. God’s knowledge is identical with God’s essence, and God knows all possible and actual individuals without species, without mental language in the creaturely sense, without discursive composition, and without really distinct faculties.

At the same time, Ockham is cautious about what reason can demonstrate concerning God. Even if one accepts by faith that God exists, Ockham does not think natural reason can demonstrate all claims about God’s free causation, knowledge, or will with the confidence some earlier theologians sought. Kilcullen notes that for Ockham it cannot be philosophically demonstrated that God causes contingently or freely, or even that he knows and wills something other than himself; such matters are held by faith.

This caution is not indifference. It is part of Ockham’s method: preserve theological commitments, but do not pretend that every theological truth is naturally demonstrable.

14. Divine omnipotence and the modal boundaries of mind

God’s mind also matters because divine omnipotence defines what is absolutely possible for created cognition. If God can cause a cognition without the ordinary created cause, then the natural order of cognition is not metaphysically necessary in the strongest possible sense. This is why intuitive cognition of non-existents matters so much.

In ordinary natural cognition, the object causes the act. In supernatural cases, God can supply the act without the object. This does not mean that ordinary cognition is worthless. It means that the created mind’s natural causal order is contingent upon divine power.

This feature has often been treated as a source of skepticism. It can be that. But it is also a theological discipline in modal metaphysics. Ockham uses God not merely as a doctrinal add-on but as the measure of what can and cannot be separated without contradiction. If cognition is an absolute quality, and if the object is not part of the intrinsic essence of that quality, then God can produce the quality without the object. The mind’s natural dependence on objects is therefore real but not absolutely inseparable.

This is one of the most characteristically Ockhamist moves: distinguish natural order from absolute possibility. Philosophy of mind, for him, must not confuse what naturally happens with what divine omnipotence could bring about.

15. Angels and separated souls: nonhuman created minds

Angels are just as important as God for reconstructing Ockham’s philosophy of mind, though in a different way. God is the simple uncreated limiting case; angels are created intellects without bodies. They show which aspects of cognition are essentially intellectual and which are tied to human embodiment.

Ockham’s Reportatio on angelic cognition is explicit: angels and human intellects do not naturally understand through species or through their own essence; rather, in intuitive cognition they understand through the very things understood. The thing present to the angelic or human intellect can cause intuitive understanding. This applies Ockham’s anti-species theory beyond human psychology. Angels do not need intelligible species any more than humans do.

Ockham also holds that angels can have abstractive cognition after intuitive cognition. Once a cognition or habit has arisen, the object need not remain present in the same way. Abstractive cognition can occur even if the object is absent or annihilated, depending on the habit or prior cognition.

This is important. Angels are not omniscient miniature gods. They are created minds with acts and possibly habits. They know created things through created cognitive acts. But because they lack sense organs, they clarify that intellectual intuition is not essentially sensory. A Spanish discussion makes the point comparatively: a separated soul or angel can have intellectual cognition without sensory cognition; human and angel can know the same singular thing by different kinds of acts, with the difference lying in the subject and mode of cognition rather than necessarily in the object known.

Angels therefore provide an intermediate case between human embodied cognition and divine simple cognition. Human cognition in the present life normally depends on sensation. Angelic cognition does not. Divine cognition depends on neither sensation nor created acts. These three cases allow Ockham to map mind across a hierarchy: embodied created mind, disembodied created mind, and uncreated simple mind.

16. The present-life dependence of human cognition on sensation

Although Ockham allows intellectual intuition and angelic cognition, he does not thereby make ordinary human cognition independent of the senses. In the present life, human intellectual cognition of external sensible things normally depends on sensory cognition. Sensible objects cause sensations; sensations are linked to intellectual intuitions; intellectual intuitions then ground contingent judgments and later abstractive cognition.

The Spanish Philosophica entry notes that intellectual intuition in the present condition is concomitant with sensory intuition, though Ockham allows that before sin, or in separated souls and angels, the relation may be different. This is a helpful corrective to over-spiritualizing Ockham. He does not make human cognition angelic. Human intellect is not naturally operating in the same way as an angelic intellect, at least in the present embodied state.

So Ockham’s position on embodiment is balanced. He denies that intellectual cognition is reducible to sensation. He allows intellectual intuition of singulars and of mental acts. He accepts the possibility of separated intellective cognition by faith and in angelology. But he also treats human cognition here and now as deeply dependent on sensory contact with singular things.

A modern label might call this “conditional embodiment”: human cognition is naturally embodied in the present life, but intellectual cognition as such is not essentially sensory.

17. Ockham against Aquinas: the decisive contrasts

Because the user asked for the seriousness that Aquinas requires, it is useful to state the contrasts directly.

Aquinas holds that the human being has one substantial form, the rational soul, which includes vegetative, sensitive, and intellective powers. Ockham tends toward a plurality of forms: corporeity, sensitive soul, and intellective soul.

Aquinas holds that intellect and will are really distinct powers of the soul. Ockham denies a real distinction and treats “intellect” and “will” as connotative terms for the same rational soul under different act-related descriptions.

Aquinas makes heavy use of species in cognition. Ockham rejects sensory and intelligible species as unnecessary explanatory entities.

Aquinas holds that the intellect directly knows universals and knows singulars only indirectly or by a return to phantasms. Ockham allows the intellect to have intuitive cognition of singulars as singulars.

Aquinas thinks the will is naturally ordered to the good in a way that limits the possibility of willing evil as evil. Ockham gives the will more radical freedom, including the possibility of choosing against the ultimate good.

Aquinas thinks the immateriality and incorruptibility of the rational soul can be philosophically demonstrated. Ockham denies that this can be strictly demonstrated by natural reason, while affirming the doctrine by faith.

The overall difference is not that Aquinas is “metaphysical” and Ockham is “empirical.” Both are metaphysical theologians. The difference is that Ockham is more ontologically parsimonious, more semantically analytical, more skeptical of demonstrative claims about the soul, more direct about singular cognition, and more radical about volitional freedom.

18. A systematic Ockhamist philosophy of mind

If we extract a full philosophy of mind from Ockham, it would include the following theses.

First, mind is act-centered. The basic mental realities are acts and habits of subjects: cognitions, volitions, judgments, passions, and dispositions. Concepts are best understood, in Ockham’s mature view, as acts of understanding functioning as natural signs.

Second, mind is ontologically austere. Do not posit species, common natures, fictive objects, or really distinct faculties unless unavoidable. Ockham’s razor operates inside psychology.

Third, human mind is hylomorphic but plural. Human beings involve corporeal, sensitive, and intellective forms. This avoids both crude materialism and simple dualism.

Fourth, intellect and will are not really distinct inner faculties. They are the same intellective soul described through different act-types. The semantic difference is real; the ontological multiplication is not.

Fifth, cognition is naturally direct and singular. External singulars naturally cause intuitive cognitions by which the intellect can form evident contingent judgments.

Sixth, abstraction is not the mind’s escape into universal things. It is cognition that abstracts from existence or non-existence and supports memory, generalization, concept-formation, and scientific habits.

Seventh, thought has linguistic structure. Mental language is composed of natural signs; spoken and written languages are conventional signs subordinated to mental concepts.

Eighth, self-knowledge is reflexive and act-directed. The intellect can intuitively know its own acts and acts of will, giving first-person access to present mental states without giving demonstrative knowledge of the soul’s metaphysical essence.

Ninth, the will is radically free. Volition is informed by cognition but not determined by cognition. Moral responsibility depends on this non-necessitation.

Tenth, affect is intentional and layered. Sensitive passions and volitional passions differ, but both are psychologically important. Emotions are not merely bodily feelings; many have cognitive and evaluative structure.

Eleventh, nonhuman minds are philosophically central. Angels and separated souls show that intellectual cognition is not essentially sensory, while human cognition in the present life remains naturally sense-dependent.

Twelfth, divine mind is the simple limiting case. God does not have faculties, species, or distinct mental acts as creatures do. “Divine intellect” and “divine will” name the same simple God under different connotative concepts.

Thirteenth, theology supplies modal depth. Divine omnipotence shows which features of cognition are naturally necessary and which are only contingently connected in the created order.

19. Major interpretive debates

Several issues remain contested in the scholarship.

The first is the status of concepts. Did Ockham at one point treat concepts as ficta, intentional objects distinct from acts, and later abandon that view? The primary texts show Ockham discussing the options, and French scholarship represented by Biard emphasizes the mature act-theory as the more rigorous Ockhamist solution.

The second is mental language. Does Ockham’s mental language eliminate synonymy and equivocation, becoming a kind of ideal logical language? Or is it more psychologically and semantically flexible? The Stanford Encyclopedia and Cambridge Companion material both note ongoing disagreement.

The third is intuitive cognition and skepticism. If God can cause intuitive cognition of a non-existent thing, does intuitive cognition still guarantee evident judgment? Some scholars preserve a strong natural connection between intuition and evidence; others weaken it. The Cambridge Companion summary of the Karger/Stump debate is especially useful here.

The fourth is the demonstrability of the soul’s status. Ockham’s refusal to demonstrate the intellective soul’s immateriality and incorruptibility has been read as skeptical, fideist, or simply methodologically rigorous. The best reading is probably the last, though his position certainly allows sharper conflicts between faith and natural reason than Aquinas would accept.

The fifth is embodiment. Ockham’s human epistemology is sense-dependent in the present life, but his theory of intellectual intuition, angelic cognition, and separated souls prevents any simple identification of mind with embodied sensation.

20. Final synthesis

Ockham’s philosophy of mind can be described as semantic, act-based, direct-realist in tendency, voluntarist, plural-hylomorphic, and theologically modal.

It is semantic because mental life is structured by natural signs and mental propositions.

It is act-based because concepts, cognitions, volitions, and passions are primarily acts or qualities of subjects, not mediating objects.

It is direct-realist in tendency because singular things naturally cause cognitions of themselves without species.

It is voluntarist because rational willing is not determined by intellectual judgment.

It is plural-hylomorphic because the human being contains corporeal, sensitive, and intellective principles.

It is theologically modal because God’s omnipotence and divine simplicity define the outer limits of what cognition, will, and mind can be.

The mind of God and the minds of angels are therefore not ornamental theological additions. They are integral to the architecture. Angels show what created intellect can be without sense organs. Separated souls show what human intellective life may be apart from the body, though known by faith rather than demonstration. God shows what intellect and will become when stripped of all creaturely composition: not faculties, not acts inhering in a subject, not mental signs, but the simple divine essence itself, named by many connotative concepts.

A concise final formula would be this: Ockham’s mind is not a container of representations; it is a subject of naturally signifying acts, evident judgments, free volitions, and intentional passions, situated within a created order whose normal causal patterns are real but whose ultimate modal boundaries are governed by divine omnipotence.

PreviousPeter John Olivi NextWalter Chatton

Digital Minds · A history of thinking about the mind, from antiquity to the present day.
A personal research project by Fabián. · About · Glossary
